title: What Makes Up an Option Premium
html:
Before you can speak the language of the Greeks, you have to understand what they are measuring sensitivities of: the option's premium. The premium is the price you pay (or collect) for the contract, and it is not a single mysterious number handed down by the market. It is the output of a handful of identifiable inputs, each of which the Greeks will later isolate. If you can decompose a premium in your head, every Greek becomes obvious — delta is just the premium's sensitivity to one of those inputs, theta to another, vega to a third.

The five-and-a-half inputs

Every standard option price is a function of the same ingredients. Memorize them, because the entire rest of this part is built on them.

  • Underlying price (S) — where the stock trades right now.
  • Strike price (K) — the fixed price at which you may buy (call) or sell (put).
  • Time to expiration (T) — how long until the contract dies, usually expressed in years.
  • Implied volatility (σ) — the market's estimate of how much the stock will move, annualized.
  • Risk-free rate (r) — the interest the cash could earn meanwhile.
  • Dividends (q) — the half-input; payouts the stock makes before expiration, which reduce a call's value.

Intrinsic value and extrinsic value

Any premium splits cleanly into two parts. The first is intrinsic value — the amount the option is already in the money. For a call that is max(S − K, 0); for a put it is max(K − S, 0). Intrinsic value is pure arithmetic; it has no opinion about the future.

Definition.
Premium = Intrinsic value + Extrinsic value. Intrinsiccall = max(S − K, 0). Everything left over — the part that pays for time and uncertainty — is extrinsic value, also called time value.

The second part, extrinsic value, is everything the market pays on top of intrinsic value. It is the price of possibility: the chance the stock keeps moving in your favor before expiration. Extrinsic value is where time (theta) and volatility (vega) live. An out-of-the-money option has zero intrinsic value, so its entire premium is extrinsic — it is pure hope with a clock running.

Decomposing a $5 premium

Suppose ACME trades at $103 and you are pricing the 100-strike call with 30 days left. The market quotes it at $5.00. Let us pull that premium apart.

Decomposing a $5.00 call premium
Underlying S = $103 Strike K = $100 Intrinsic = max(103 − 100, 0) = $3.00 Premium quoted = $5.00 Extrinsic = 5.00 − 3.00 = $2.00 So you pay $3 of certainty and $2 of time + volatility

That $2.00 of extrinsic value is the battleground for the rest of this part. Raise implied volatility and it grows (vega). Let a week pass with the stock still and it shrinks (theta). Push the stock higher and the whole premium climbs because intrinsic value rises faster than extrinsic erodes (delta and gamma). The Greeks are simply the dials that tell you how fast each of those things happens.

How each input pushes the premium

Input risesCall premiumPut premium
Underlying S ↑updown
Time T ↑upup
Volatility σ ↑upup
Rate r ↑updown
Dividend q ↑downup

Notice that time and volatility lift both calls and puts — more time and more movement mean more chance of finishing in the money, regardless of direction. Direction-dependent inputs (S, r, q) help one side and hurt the other. Each row of this table will become a Greek with a name and a number.

Insight.
An option is a decaying asset wrapped around a stock position. The intrinsic part behaves like the stock; the extrinsic part behaves like a melting ice cube. The Greeks measure the melting.

One more visual will anchor the idea. The curve below shows how a call's total value bends above its intrinsic-value kink. The gap between the smooth curve and the hard hockey-stick is extrinsic value — widest at the money, vanishing deep in or deep out.

Read it carefully. Far to the left, the call is worthless and the curve hugs zero. Far to the right, the curve runs parallel to and just above the intrinsic line — deep-ITM options are almost all intrinsic value with little time premium left. Right at the strike, the bulge is largest: that is where uncertainty is richest and where, as we will see, gamma and vega concentrate.

▸Try It

Decompose three live premiums into intrinsic and extrinsic value.

  1. Pick a liquid stock and note its price S.
  2. For an ITM, an ATM, and an OTM call in the same expiration, write down each premium.
  3. Compute intrinsic = max(S − K, 0) for each, then subtract to get extrinsic value.
  4. Confirm the OTM option is 100% extrinsic and that extrinsic value is largest for the near-the-money strike.

title: Delta: Sensitivity, Hedge Ratio and Probability
html:
Delta is the first Greek every trader meets, and the most useful, because it answers three different questions at once. Formally it is the rate of change of the option's value with respect to the underlying price — the slope of the premium curve you saw in the last lesson. But that single slope can be read as a price sensitivity, a hedge ratio, and a rough probability. Mastering delta means holding all three readings in your head simultaneously.

Definition.
Δ = ∂V ÷ ∂S — the change in option value per $1 change in the underlying. Calls have delta from 0 to +1; puts from 0 to −1. By convention quotes often show delta as a whole number, so 0.60 is “60 delta.”

Reading one: price sensitivity

The plainest reading is mechanical. A call with delta 0.60 gains about $0.60 in value for every $1 the stock rises, and loses about $0.60 for every $1 it falls. Since one equity contract controls 100 shares, that 0.60 delta means roughly $60 of P&L per $1 stock move per contract.

0.60-delta call: a $1 move
Stock moves $50 → $51 (+$1) Premium change ≈ Δ × ΔS = 0.60 × 1 = +$0.60 Per contract (×100) = +$60 If stock falls to $49 instead = −$60

Deep-in-the-money calls approach delta 1.00 — they track the stock almost dollar-for-dollar, behaving like stock itself. Deep-out-of-the-money calls have delta near 0.00 — the stock can wiggle and the premium barely notices. The curve below plots delta across the underlying.

The S-shape is the whole story. The curve is flat at both extremes — far OTM and far ITM, delta barely changes — and steepest right at the money, where it passes through about 0.50. That steepness is gamma, the subject of the next lesson. For now, note that an at-the-money option is the most responsive place on the curve: its delta is changing fastest as the stock moves.

Reading two: the hedge ratio

Delta also tells you how many shares replicate — or neutralize — the option. If your 0.60-delta call behaves like 60 shares of long stock, you can cancel its directional risk by shorting 60 shares. Do that and small moves in the stock leave your combined position roughly flat: this is delta hedging, the foundation of how dealers warehouse risk.

Insight.
A long call at 0.60 delta plus 60 short shares is delta-neutral for an instant. As the stock moves, the call's delta changes but the share count does not, so the hedge drifts — which is exactly why gamma matters and why hedges must be rebalanced.

Reading three: a rough probability

The third reading is the most quoted and the most abused. A call's delta is approximately the risk-neutral probability that the option finishes in the money. A 0.60-delta call is loosely “about 60% likely” to expire ITM; a 0.20-delta put is “about 20% likely.” This is why traders selling premium speak of selling the “10-delta strike” — they mean a strike with roughly a 10% chance of being breached.

Pitfall.
Delta-as-probability is an approximation, not gospel. It is the risk-neutral probability, not the real-world one, and it ignores the difference between finishing a penny ITM and finishing deep ITM. Use it as a quick gauge, never as a precise forecast.

Putting all three together: a 0.60-delta call gains ~$0.60 per $1 up-move (sensitivity), can be hedged with 60 short shares (hedge ratio), and is roughly 60% likely to finish in the money (probability). One number, three lenses.

Position delta

Delta is additive, which makes it the master gauge of a whole book. Long 5 of those calls is +300 deltas (5 × 0.60 × 100). Add 100 short shares (−100 deltas) and your net is +200 deltas — you are effectively long 200 shares regardless of how many legs you hold. Every multi-leg position collapses to a single net-delta number that tells you your directional exposure at a glance.

▸Try It

Verify delta's three meanings on a real option chain.

  1. Find an ATM call and confirm its delta is near 0.50.
  2. Estimate its premium change for a $1 stock move using Δ × 1, then watch the quote when the stock actually moves a dollar.
  3. Write down how many shares you would short to delta-hedge one contract.
  4. Compare its delta to the OTM strikes and sanity-check the “probability of finishing ITM” reading.

title: Gamma: How Delta Changes
html:
If delta is speed, gamma is acceleration. Delta told you how fast the premium moves when the stock moves; gamma tells you how fast delta itself changes. It is the second derivative of the option value with respect to price, and it is the reason a hedge that is perfect at this instant is wrong an instant later. Understanding gamma is what separates traders who merely know delta from traders who can manage a position through a fast market.

Definition.
Γ = ∂Δ ÷ ∂S = ∂2V ÷ ∂S2. It measures how much delta changes per $1 move in the underlying. Gamma is identical for a call and the same-strike put, and it is always positive for long options.

Where gamma lives

Recall the S-shaped delta curve from the last lesson. Gamma is its slope. Where the delta curve is steepest — right at the money — gamma is highest. Where the delta curve flattens out — deep ITM or deep OTM — gamma falls toward zero. So gamma is a hump centered on the strike.

The bell shape carries two practical truths. First, at-the-money options have the most gamma, so their deltas are the most unstable — a small move flips them around quickly. Second, deep-ITM and deep-OTM options have almost no gamma; their deltas are pinned near 1.0 or 0.0 and barely budge. Gamma also rises as expiration approaches for ATM options, which is why the last day of a near-the-money option is so violent.

A worked example: delta swinging on a $2 move

NOVA trades at $50. The 50-strike call is at-the-money with delta 0.50 and gamma 0.075. The stock rallies $2 to $52. How does delta respond?

Delta moving because of gamma
Starting delta = 0.50 Gamma = 0.075 per $1 Stock move = +$2 Delta change ≈ Γ × ΔS = 0.075 × 2 = +0.15 New delta ≈ 0.50 + 0.15 = 0.65

The delta swung from 0.50 to 0.65 purely because gamma is high near the money. That is the example in the skeleton, and it shows why gamma is the trader's friend when long and the trader's enemy when short. A long-gamma position gets longer as the stock rises and shorter as it falls — it automatically leans into winners. A short-gamma position does the opposite, getting shorter into rallies and longer into selloffs, which is precisely the wrong way to be positioned in a trending move.

Insight.
Long options are long gamma: your delta improves in your favor as the stock moves, in either direction. That convexity is what you pay for with theta. Gamma and theta are two sides of one trade — you cannot have positive gamma without paying time decay.

Gamma and the cost of hedging

Return to the delta-hedged call from the last lesson: long one 0.60-delta call, short 60 shares. The stock rises and the call's delta climbs to, say, 0.70. Now you are net +10 deltas — the hedge slipped because gamma moved your delta. To re-neutralize you must short 10 more shares, and you do so at a higher price. If the stock then falls, your delta drops back and you buy shares back lower. Long gamma means you are structurally selling high and buying low as you rebalance — the rebalancing itself harvests value, which is what offsets the theta you pay.

Pitfall.
Short-gamma positions (selling options) force the opposite: you rebalance by buying high and selling low. In a quiet market the theta you collect wins; in a sharp move the gamma losses can dwarf weeks of collected premium. Never judge a short-premium book by its theta alone — check its gamma.

Reading gamma on a position

Like delta, gamma is additive across a book. A market maker's whole job is often described as managing net gamma: long-gamma books love volatility and hate stillness; short-gamma books love stillness and fear gaps. When you hear that “dealers are short gamma below this strike,” it means their forced hedging amplifies moves — selling into weakness — which is why gamma positioning has become a widely watched market-structure signal.

▸Try It

See gamma reshape delta on a real chain.

  1. Note the delta and gamma of an ATM option and a deep-ITM option in the same expiration.
  2. Confirm the ATM gamma is far larger than the deep-ITM gamma.
  3. Estimate the new delta after a $2 move using Δnew ≈ Δ + Γ × 2.
  4. Repeat for a contract one day from expiration and watch how much higher ATM gamma has become.

title: Theta: Time Decay
html:
Every option is a wasting asset. The extrinsic value you isolated in Lesson 1 — the premium paid for time and uncertainty — bleeds away as expiration approaches, because there is steadily less time left for the stock to move in your favor. Theta is the Greek that puts a number on that bleed: how much value an option loses per day, all else equal. For option buyers theta is rent; for option sellers it is income.

Definition.
Θ = ∂V ÷ ∂t — the change in option value as one day passes, holding everything else constant. It is quoted as a negative number for long options (e.g. −0.05 means the option loses $0.05 per day, or $5 per contract).

Quantifying the daily bleed

Theta is the easiest Greek to feel in your account. Consider an at-the-money option carrying a theta of −0.05. Hold it overnight with the stock unchanged and you wake up $0.05 poorer per share, $5 poorer per contract.

Theta bleed over a quiet week
Theta = −0.05 / day Per contract (×100) = −$5 / day 5 calendar days, stock flat = −$25 Long 10 contracts = −$250 for the week Note: this is the cost if NOTHING moves

That is the buyer's predicament: you can be right about direction and still lose if you are slow, because theta charges rent every single day. The seller's mirror image is that collecting that $25 per contract is the entire business model of premium selling — as long as the stock does not move enough to overwhelm it.

Theta accelerates into expiration

The crucial subtlety is that theta is not constant. For an at-the-money option, time decay accelerates as expiration nears. Extrinsic value does not melt linearly; it follows a curve that drops gently far from expiration and then plunges in the final stretch. The decay of an ATM option is roughly proportional to the square root of time remaining, so the last weeks shed value far faster than the first.

Read the curve from right (lots of time) to left (expiration). Far out, the line is nearly flat — a 90-day ATM option barely decays day to day. As you approach the final two to three weeks, the curve steepens sharply, and in the last few days it falls off a cliff. This is why an ATM option that is −0.05 today might be −0.12 with a week to go, even though nothing about the stock changed.

Insight.
The accelerating curve is why sellers often favor the 30-to-45-day window and close around 7-14 days left: they collect the steepest part of the decay, then step aside before gamma risk explodes. Buyers face the reverse incentive — buy more time than you think you need, because the cheapest decay is far from expiry.

Theta depends on moneyness

Where the option sits matters. At-the-money options have the most extrinsic value to lose, so they carry the largest theta and the most dramatic terminal acceleration. Deep-ITM and deep-OTM options have little extrinsic value left, so their theta is small — there simply isn't much time premium to bleed. An OTM option's decay also accelerates, but because it is decaying toward zero, the final collapse can wipe out the whole premium.

Pitfall.
Theta and gamma move together but with opposite signs. The same ATM option whose theta accelerates into expiration also has gamma exploding in that window. Sellers harvesting fat late-cycle theta are simultaneously taking on dangerous gamma — one overnight gap can erase a month of collected decay.

Theta is paid for vega and gamma

Never read theta in isolation. The decay you pay as a buyer is the price of two good things: positive gamma (your delta improves as the stock moves) and positive vega (you profit if volatility rises). A long straddle bleeds theta every day precisely because it is long gamma and long vega. The question is never “is theta bad?” but “am I being paid enough in movement and volatility to cover the rent?”

▸Try It

Measure theta acceleration directly.

  1. Pick an ATM option ~45 days out and record its theta and premium.
  2. Find the same strike ~10 days out and record its theta.
  3. Confirm the near-dated theta is meaningfully larger (more negative).
  4. Multiply each theta by 100 to express the daily dollar bleed per contract, and project the cost of holding a week.

title: Vega: Sensitivity to Implied Volatility
html:
Vega is the Greek that catches new traders off guard. They buy a call, the stock rallies exactly as predicted, and the option still loses money. The culprit is almost always vega — the option's sensitivity to implied volatility, the market's estimate of future movement baked into the premium. You can be right on direction and timing and still lose if implied volatility collapses underneath you. Vega measures exactly how much.

Definition.
ν = ∂V ÷ ∂σ — the change in option value per 1 percentage-point change in implied volatility. (Vega is not a true Greek letter, but it is universal.) A vega of 0.12 means the premium gains $0.12 if IV rises one point and loses $0.12 if IV falls one point.

Estimating a price change from an IV shift

Vega is a simple multiplier. Take an option with vega 0.12. If implied volatility rises from 30% to 35% — a 5-point increase — the premium gains about 0.12 × 5 = $0.60, with the stock perfectly still. Volatility alone moved the price.

Vega: a 5-point IV rise
Vega = 0.12 per IV point IV moves 30% → 35% (+5 pts) Premium change ≈ ν × Δσ = 0.12 × 5 = +$0.60 Per contract (×100) = +$60 Stock did not move at all

The earnings IV crush

Nowhere is vega more brutal than around earnings. Implied volatility inflates in the days before a report as the market prices in the coming surprise, then collapses the instant the news is out and uncertainty resolves. This is the famous IV crush. Suppose you hold a $4.00 ATM call with vega 0.12 into earnings, where IV stands at 60%. The report lands, the stock barely moves, and IV crashes from 60% to 35% — a 25-point drop.

Earnings IV crush guts the premium
Premium before = $4.00 Vega = 0.12 IV drop = 60% → 35% = −25 pts Vega P&L ≈ 0.12 × (−25) = −$3.00 Premium after (stock flat) = ≈ $1.00 You lost 75% with the stock unchanged

That is the trap: the stock did exactly nothing, yet three-quarters of the premium vaporized because the volatility you paid for evaporated. Long-premium earnings plays must clear not just the stock move but the vega drag of the crush. Sellers, by contrast, position for the crush — selling rich pre-earnings premium and buying it back cheap after.

Pitfall.
Buying options into earnings “because the stock will move” ignores that IV crush is nearly guaranteed while the move is not. The break-even move often has to exceed the entire implied move just to overcome vega losses. Always check vega and the IV level before an event trade.

Where vega is largest

Vega, like gamma, peaks at the money — but unlike gamma it grows with time to expiration. Long-dated options have far more vega than weeklies, because a change in the volatility assumption compounds over more time. A LEAPS option is dominated by vega; a one-day option has almost none.

The hump shape mirrors gamma, but the time relationship is reversed: gamma is biggest for short-dated ATM options, vega for long-dated ones. This is why calendar spreads — selling a short-dated option and buying a long-dated one at the same strike — are net long vega: the long leg's vega outweighs the short leg's. If implied volatility rises, the calendar profits even though it is short an option.

Insight.
Think of vega as your bet on the volatility regime. Long vega wants fear and uncertainty to grow; short vega wants calm. Many blow-ups happen when a trader is unknowingly short vega — collecting theta in a quiet market — and a volatility spike repriced every option against them at once.
▸Try It

Catch an IV crush in the wild.

  1. Find a stock reporting earnings this week and note the ATM option's IV and vega the day before.
  2. Estimate the premium loss if IV falls 20 points: ν × (−20).
  3. After the report, record the new IV and premium with the stock roughly flat.
  4. Compare your vega estimate to the actual premium drop.

title: Rho: Sensitivity to Interest Rates
html:
Rho is the forgotten Greek. For most short-dated retail trades it is so small it can be ignored entirely, which is why it gets the least attention. But it is part of the complete picture, and for long-dated options — LEAPS, deep-ITM substitutes for stock, large institutional books — it can matter quite a bit. Rho measures the option's sensitivity to the risk-free interest rate.

Definition.
ρ = ∂V ÷ ∂r — the change in option value per 1 percentage-point change in the risk-free interest rate. Calls have positive rho (they rise when rates rise); puts have negative rho (they fall when rates rise).

Why rates touch option prices at all

The intuition flows from the carry of the underlying. Buying a call is a leveraged way to control stock without paying the full cash up front — you keep your cash and let it earn interest. The higher the interest rate, the more valuable that deferral of payment is, so calls are worth more when rates are high. A put is the reverse: it defers receiving cash from a sale, so higher rates make it slightly less attractive, giving puts negative rho.

Why rho is a long-dated phenomenon

Rho scales with time, because interest is earned over time. The cost of carry on a one-week option covers seven days of interest — trivial. The cost of carry on a two-year LEAPS covers two years — substantial. So rho is negligible for weeklies and meaningful for LEAPS. Compare a LEAPS call to a weekly when rates climb a full point.

Rho: a 1-point rate rise, two horizons
Rates rise 4% → 5% (+1 pt) LEAPS call rho = ~0.45 LEAPS value change ≈ 0.45 × 1 = +$0.45 (per share) Weekly call rho = ~0.01 Weekly value change ≈ 0.01 × 1 = +$0.01 (negligible)

The LEAPS gains a real, if modest, $0.45 per share from the rate move; the weekly's penny is lost in the bid-ask noise. This is the entire practical lesson of rho: it is a slow, long-horizon Greek that only earns a seat at the table when expiration is far away.

The curve shows rho increasing as the option moves into the money — deep-ITM calls have the most rho because they behave most like a financed stock position, carrying the largest deferred purchase. Notice how much steeper the long-dated curve sits above the short-dated one; that vertical gap is the time-scaling of rho.

Insight.
In a stable-rate environment rho fades into irrelevance, which is why it earns the least screen time. But during a rate-hiking or rate-cutting cycle, long-dated option books feel rho directly — and the put/call rho asymmetry can shift the relative pricing of bullish versus bearish long-term structures.

Rho in practice

For the vast majority of trades — anything inside a couple of months — you can safely set rho aside and concentrate on delta, gamma, theta, and vega. Where to keep it in mind:

  • LEAPS and stock-replacement calls — a deep-ITM long-dated call used instead of buying shares carries real rho; rising rates quietly help it.
  • Box spreads and synthetic financing — these are essentially interest-rate trades, and rho is their dominant Greek.
  • Macro regime shifts — when the central bank is actively moving rates, repricing of carry flows through rho across long-dated chains.
Pitfall.
Do not confuse rho with the indirect effect of rates on stocks. A rate hike can crush equity prices — but that hits your option through delta, not rho. Rho is the direct, all-else-equal carry effect only. Keep the two channels separate when you reason about a rate decision.
▸Try It

Compare rho across expirations.

  1. Pull up an ATM call expiring this week and a LEAPS call on the same stock.
  2. Record the rho of each (your platform's Greeks tab will list it).
  3. Estimate each option's value change for a 1-point rate move using ρ × 1.
  4. Confirm the weekly's rho is near zero while the LEAPS' is materially larger, and check that the put rho carries the opposite sign.

title: Black-Scholes: Intuition and the Formula
html:
Every Greek you have met so far is a partial derivative of one equation: the Black-Scholes-Merton option pricing formula. You do not need to derive it, but you should be able to read it, because it ties the whole part together — each Greek is simply Black-Scholes differentiated with respect to one input. This lesson presents the intuition, the formula, and the direct link from the formula to delta, gamma, theta, vega, and rho.

The intuition

Black-Scholes answers a single question: what is the fair price today of the right to buy a stock at strike K at a future date, given how much the stock is likely to wander? Its core insight is that you can perfectly replicate an option by continuously holding a precise amount of the stock (its delta) financed with borrowed cash. If a portfolio of stock-plus-borrowing exactly reproduces the option's payoff, then by no-arbitrage the option must cost the same as that portfolio. The formula is the closed-form price of that replicating portfolio.

Formula.
Call price C = S·N(d1) − K·e−rT·N(d2), where d1 = [ln(S÷K) + (r + σ2÷2)T] ÷ (σ√T) and d2 = d1 − σ√T. N(·) is the cumulative normal distribution.

Read it in two pieces. The first term, S·N(d1), is the expected value of receiving the stock if the option finishes in the money. The second term, K·e−rT·N(d2), is the present value of paying the strike, weighted by the probability N(d2) that you actually exercise. The call is worth the discounted difference: what you get minus what you pay, each scaled by its probability. The e−rT discount factor is where rho comes from; the N(d1) term is, remarkably, the delta.

A full worked pricing

Let us price the contract from the skeleton: S = $100, K = $100 (at the money), T = 30 days (≈ 0.0822 years), σ = 30%, r = 5%.

Black-Scholes step by step
σ√T = 0.30 × √0.0822 = 0.0860 ln(S/K) = ln(1) = 0 (r + σ²/2)T = (0.05 + 0.045)(0.0822) = 0.00781 d1 = (0 + 0.00781) / 0.0860 = 0.0908 d2 = 0.0908 − 0.0860 = 0.0048 N(d1) = 0.5362   N(d2) = 0.5019 C = 100(0.5362) − 100·e−0.00411(0.5019) C = 53.62 − 49.99 = $3.63

So the fair value of this 30-day ATM call is about $3.63. And we got delta for free: N(d1) = 0.536 is the call's delta. An at-the-money call has a delta slightly above 0.50 — nudged up by the drift term (r + σ2÷2) — exactly as the delta lesson promised.

Every Greek is a derivative of this formula

Here is the unifying idea. Each Greek is Black-Scholes differentiated with respect to one input, and most have clean closed forms built from the same N(d1), N(d2), and the normal density n(d1).

GreekClosed form (call)
Delta ΔN(d1)
Gamma Γn(d1) ÷ (S·σ√T)
Vega νS·n(d1)·√T
Theta Θ−[S·n(d1)·σ ÷ (2√T)] − rK·e−rTN(d2)
Rho ρK·T·e−rT·N(d2)

Look how the formulas confirm everything from earlier lessons. Gamma and vega both contain n(d1), the bell-shaped normal density that peaks at the money — which is precisely why gamma and vega are humps centered on the strike. Vega carries a √T factor, so it grows with time; the first theta term carries 1÷√T, so decay accelerates as T shrinks. Rho carries the T·e−rT factor, so it scales with time-to-expiry. The shapes you saw in the curves are not coincidences — they are these formulas drawn out.

Insight.
You will almost never compute Black-Scholes by hand in live trading — your platform does it instantly. The value of reading the formula is conceptual: it shows that the Greeks are not five unrelated rules of thumb but five views of one surface. Change an input, and the formula and all its derivatives update together.
Pitfall.
Black-Scholes assumes constant volatility, lognormal returns, no jumps, and European exercise. Real markets violate all four — which is exactly why implied volatility is not flat across strikes. That gap between the model's assumptions and reality is the subject of the final lesson.

One last way to see it: the curve above is C as a function of S. Its slope at any point is delta, and the rate the slope changes is gamma. The smoothness that distinguishes this curve from the sharp payoff hockey-stick is the time and volatility value that Black-Scholes prices — and that the Greeks dissect.

▸Try It

Reproduce a quoted price with Black-Scholes.

  1. Take a liquid ATM option and record S, K, days to expiry, the quoted IV, and a current short rate.
  2. Use any Black-Scholes calculator to price it with those inputs.
  3. Compare the model price to the market mid — they should be close.
  4. Read N(d1) off the calculator and confirm it matches the listed delta.

title: Implied Volatility, the Smile and Managing a Position
html:
The final lesson closes the loop. Implied volatility is the one Black-Scholes input the market does not give you directly — it is reverse-engineered from the option's price — and its behavior across strikes (the smile and skew) reveals where the model breaks and where opportunity lives. Then we put every Greek to work managing a real multi-leg position into an event. This is where the Greeks stop being definitions and become a control panel.

Implied volatility is the market's price, restated

Of the six Black-Scholes inputs, five are observable: S, K, T, r, and dividends. The sixth, volatility, is not — nobody knows the future. So traders run Black-Scholes backwards: they take the option's market price and solve for the volatility that makes the model match it. That number is the implied volatility. It is not a forecast handed down from on high; it is simply the market's price expressed in volatility units.

Definition.
Implied volatility is the value of σ that, plugged into Black-Scholes, reproduces the option's observed market price. High IV means rich premiums and an expensive option; low IV means cheap premiums. IV is the great common language for comparing option prices across strikes, stocks, and time.

The smile and the skew

Black-Scholes assumes one volatility for all strikes. The market disagrees. If you plot implied volatility against strike, you do not get a flat line — you get a curve. For many assets it is a smile (IV higher for both deep-OTM puts and deep-OTM calls) and for equity indices it is a skew (IV markedly higher for downside puts than for upside calls).

Read the curve from left (low strikes, downside puts) to right (high strikes, upside calls). The left side sits high — that is the equity skew. It exists because crashes are faster and deeper than rallies, and because investors pay up for downside protection, bidding put IV higher. The dip near the money is where IV is lowest; the right side ticks up again into the smile. The practical takeaway: a 10-delta put is usually priced at a higher implied volatility than a 10-delta call — downside insurance is structurally expensive.

Insight.
Skew is information. Steepening put skew signals rising fear and demand for crash protection; flattening skew signals complacency. Vertical spreads, risk reversals, and ratio trades are all ways of taking a view on the shape of the skew, not just the level of IV.

Managing a position with net Greeks

Now the payoff. Because every Greek is additive, a complex position collapses to a handful of net numbers that you steer like a dashboard. Consider an iron-condor-style position held into earnings: short an OTM call spread and short an OTM put spread. Each leg has its own Greeks; what you manage is the net.

Net Greeks of a short-premium condor into earnings
Net delta = −5 (slight short bias, near neutral) Net gamma = −30 (short gamma — a gap hurts) Net theta = +$45 / day (collecting decay) Net vega = −180 (short vega — wants IV crush) Read: profits from decay + crush, loses on a big move

This single readout tells the whole story. The position is roughly delta-neutral, so direction is not the main bet. It is long theta (+$45/day): it makes money simply by time passing. It is short vega (−180): the earnings IV crush from, say, 60% to 35% would hand it 25 × 180 = $4,500 of vega profit. But it is short gamma (−30): if the stock gaps hard on the report, the net delta swings violently against the move and losses pile up fast. The trade's thesis, stated in Greeks, is “volatility is overpriced into the event and the stock will not gap beyond the short strikes.”

Do this.
Before any multi-leg trade, write its four net Greeks on one line: delta (direction), gamma (move risk), theta (time edge), vega (vol edge). If you cannot state your thesis in those four numbers, you do not yet understand the position you are about to put on.

Steering the position

Managing the trade is managing those numbers. If the stock drifts and net delta grows to −25, you buy stock or add a call to flatten it back toward neutral. If you decide the IV crush has already happened and you no longer want short vega, you buy back a wing. If gamma risk feels too large into the print, you reduce size or buy a cheap further-OTM option to cap the tail. Every adjustment is a deliberate change to one net Greek.

Pitfall.
The most common blow-up is loving the theta and ignoring the gamma and vega beside it. A short-premium book looks like free money on quiet days — positive theta ticking in — right up until a gap or a vol spike triggers the short gamma and short vega simultaneously. Always size to the move you are short, not to the decay you are collecting.

That is the whole part in one sentence: an option premium is built from price, time, and volatility (Lesson 1); delta, gamma, theta, vega, and rho measure its sensitivity to each (Lessons 2-6); Black-Scholes ties them into one surface (Lesson 7); and implied volatility plus the net Greeks let you read, price, and steer any position you hold (Lesson 8).

▸Try It

Build and read a net-Greek dashboard.

  1. Pull the IV of several strikes on one expiration and sketch the smile/skew — confirm downside puts carry higher IV.
  2. Construct a paper iron condor and record each leg's delta, gamma, theta, and vega.
  3. Sum them into four net Greeks and state the trade's thesis in one sentence using those numbers.
  4. Decide in advance which Greek you would adjust, and how, if the stock moved toward one of your short strikes.
